data.title: FFLG: Performance Disappointments Outweigh GARP Tilt, A Hold
data.description:
Today, I would like to reassess the Fidelity Fundamental Large Cap Growth ETF (FFLG), the actively managed exchange-traded fund I initiated coverage of in 2024. I opted for a neutral tone back then, as FFLG did not make an impression necessary for a Buy rating: it was performing nicely but not strongly enough, lagging certain peers. Plus, its history with a new strategy in place was too short to draw more or less confident conclusions about its possible potential.

However, my dear readers have likely noticed that this year, my regularly skeptical tone on growth-centered ETFs and that factor overall has changed, with the recent note on the Fidelity Enhanced Large Cap Growth ETF (FELG), for instance, marking an upgrade. But while I finally found a reason for optimism about some names in this arena, I remain neutral on others, FFLG included. What do I not like about it?

There are a few points of concern, insufficient, of course, for a bearish thesis that, among other things, would be untimely given the market environment, yet still enough to maintain the Hold rating. Among them are its underperformance versus a few peers (the key problem), relatively high expense ratio, and some minor issues in terms of liquidity. Obviously, there are more aspects to discuss, which we will be doing below.

FFLG Key Facts

  • Inception: February 2, 2021
  • Sponsor: Fidelity Management & Research Company LLC
  • AUM: $529.3 million, as of January 11
  • Expense Ratio: 0.38%
  • Turnover: 52%

FFLG Strategy

The essential thing not to miss is that, incepted in 2021, FFLG underwent quite a profound strategy change in 2024, rendering its performance delivered prior to that irrelevant for our analysis. As we know from the press release by Fidelity Investments®, before February 26, 2024, the ETF was known as the Fidelity® Growth Opportunities ETF and traded with the ticker FGRO. Another nuance is that FGRO was an active, semi-transparent ETF, while FFLG is fully transparent. Besides, the expense ratio was cut from 59 bps to 38 bps.

So what is FFLG's strategy now? As explained on the Fidelity website, the focus is on large-cap names that "...have above-average growth potential." It is clarified that it "...may be measured by factors such as earnings or revenue." More specifically:

In buying and selling securities for the fund, the Adviser starts with fundamental analyst research and security recommendations, and reference portfolios managed by the Adviser that are based on fundamental analysis, which involves a bottom-up assessment of a company's potential for success in light of fundamental factors such as its financial condition, earnings outlook, strategy, management, industry position, and economic and market conditions.

The next step is

...a quantitative portfolio construction process designed to emphasize securities in which the Adviser has high conviction subject to appropriate security and portfolio-level risk, liquidity, and trading characteristics.

FFLG Performance: Mild Disappointments

FFLG's performance since the strategy change is quite healthy, and there is a lot to admire about it, yet I believe it has not been robust enough to justify its higher fee compared to simpler, passively managed vehicles like the Schwab U.S. Large-Cap Growth ETF™ (SCHG) and the Invesco NASDAQ 100 ETF (QQQM), which have the ERs at 4 bps and 15 bps, respectively.

First, since the strategy change in February 2024, FFLG has delivered a total return that easily dwarfs those of the iShares Core S&P 500 ETF (IVV) and the iShares Russell 1000 ETF (IWB).

However, this aura of outperformance dissipates when such vehicles as SCHG, QQQM, the iShares Russell 1000 Growth ETF (IWF), and the Fidelity Enhanced Large Cap Growth ETF are added to the comparison. The fact is, FFLG was not very successful in competing with them, for instance, trailing QQQM by about 95 bps.

Second, deeper issues can be found when it comes to risk metrics.

The gist is that over the March 2024–December 2025 period, FFLG delivered much higher volatility than SCHG, QQQM, and FELG, which resulted in it having the weakest risk-adjusted returns in the group (the Sharpe and Sortino ratios). And even though IVV and IWB delivered lower annualized returns, they still had much stronger risk-adjusted performance.

Other issues that feed my skepticism are the maximum drawdown FFLG had over the period (delivered in April 2025), the deepest in the group, as well as its downside capture ratio of close to 130%.

FFLG Portfolio

As of January 8, there were 96 equities (including common stocks and ADRs) in the FFLG portfolio, with the key ten accounting for about 58.2% of the net assets. Other asset classes represented were a central fund, currency, and futures contract, with a net weight of 69 bps.

In this basket, I see a lot of S&P 500 constituents, so the overlap with the IVV portfolio is quite large.

Parameter FFLG IVV
Stocks present in both FFLG and IVV portfolios 83.25% 49.16%

Calculated by the author using data from the ETFs

Looking at the key ten companies in this portfolio, we see a rather familiar overallocation to the Magnificent Seven names typical for most large-cap growth ETFs.

Company Ticker Weight in FFLG, as of January 8 Weight in IVV, as of January 9 Sector Market Cap, $ Billion, as of January 11
NVIDIA Corporation (NVDA) 15.01% 7.54% Information Technology 4,491.91
Alphabet Inc. (GOOGL) 5.77% 3.21% Communication 3,967.94
Alphabet Inc. (GOOG) 2.81% 2.57% Communication 3,967.94
Apple Inc. (AAPL) 8.05% 6.43% Information Technology 3,812.20
Microsoft Corporation (MSFT) 7.49% 5.98% Information Technology 3,562.19
Amazon.com, Inc. (AMZN) 6.42% 4.04% Consumer Discretionary 2,644.55
Meta Platforms, Inc. (META) 4.60% 2.39% Communication 1,646.06
Broadcom Inc. (AVGO) 3.43% 2.73% Information Technology 1,635.60
Tesla, Inc. (TSLA) 1.57% 2.10% Consumer Discretionary 1,480.02
Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company Limited (TSM) 2.63% N/A Information Technology 1,377.43
Eli Lilly and Company (LLY) 1.51% 1.42% Health Care 952.29

Created by the author using data from Seeking Alpha and the ETFs

Interestingly, FFLG is overweight in all these names except for TSLA and TSM, with the case with the latter being that it is not represented in IVV at all.

It is also of note that while FFLG is clearly U.S.-focused, it does have exposure to non-U.S. names, including Tencent Holdings Limited (TCEHY), represented by an ADR, and Aritzia Inc. (ATZAF), represented by the Toronto-quoted common stock (ATZ:CA), to name a few.

FFLG Sector Mix

For an active growth ETF, FFLG has a sector mix unsurprisingly heavy in IT and communication that together account for about 63.7% of its net assets.

At the same time, a few differences between sector preferences of IVV, SCHG, and FFLG can be spotted upon a deeper inspection.

  • Compared to IVV, FFLG is overweight in IT, communication, and consumer discretionary. It is underweight in all other eight GICS sectors, mainly in financials.
  • Compared to SCHG, FFLG is overweight in five sectors, mostly in IT and communication. The main sectors it is underweight in are health care and financials. Their exposures to utilities are on par (its weight in FFLG is just 2 bps larger).

FFLG Factor Mix

My opinion is that the FFLG portfolio's factor mix has quite a lot of advantages over that of SCHG, chiefly in terms of growth (i.e., the weighted average metrics like forward revenue growth rate, etc.) and quality (including Return on Assets, Return on Total Capital, etc.) Still, both, of course, have vulnerabilities when it comes to valuation, which is quite vividly illustrated by the fact that they have less than 3% allocations to stocks with a B- Quant Valuation rating or higher.

Nevertheless, valuation is a nuanced matter, and when the growth-at-a-reasonable-price characteristics analysis is incorporated, some quite surprising findings can be arrived at.

FFLG GARP Characteristics

So to assess the GARP aspect, I selected three metrics I have been using quite often recently, shown in the table below.

Metrics FFLG SCHG
PEG Ratio 1.075 1.318
Price/Sales-To-Growth Ratio 0.490 0.578
EV/EBITDA-To-Growth Ratio 0.758 0.972

Calculated by the author using data from Seeking Alpha and the ETFs

Here, we see that the FFLG is clearly ahead of its passively managed competitor. In other words, investors get more growth exposure for every dollar they invest in the FFLG portfolio compared to that of SCHG.

Are FFLG's GARP characteristics driven by NVDA only?

I would concur here that there is a temptation to hastily attribute these differences to the fact that FFLG is overweight in NVDA (15.01% weight vs. 10.837% in SCHG), one of the key examples of a GARP stock in today's market. The metrics below speak quite loudly in favor of this hypothesis.

Metric NVDA
Fwd EPS Growth Rate 80.07%
Fwd Revenue Growth Rate 73.80%
Fwd EBITDA Growth Rate 85.18%
PEG (P/E TTM divided by Fwd EPS Growth Rate) 0.57
Price/Sales-To-Growth 0.33
EV/EBITDA-To-Growth 0.46

Based on data from Seeking Alpha. GARP metrics were computed by the author.

Still, my guess was that the story here is more complex, so I decided to delve deeper into that matter and prepared the same metrics for a hypothetical portfolio that has the exact same equity holdings as FFLG but with NVDA's weight trimmed to 10.837% and the remaining 4.173% redistributed between other equities. And it turned out that even though the weighted average market cap would drop to $1.86 trillion and the forward EPS growth rate would be lower at 32.98%, such a portfolio would have also had an advantage over SCHG when it comes to the GARP metrics.

Metric Hypothetical Portfolio SCHG
PEG 1.141 1.318
Price/Sales-To-Growth 0.515 0.578
EV/EBITDA-To-Growth 0.794 0.972

Calculated by the author using data from Seeking Alpha and the ETFs

So what companies that did not qualify for the SCHG but were selected by FFLG contribute to these metrics? I would like to present the key five, namely the following:

Stock Weight in FFLG Sector PEG P/S-to Growth EV/EBITDA-to-Growth
Micron Technology, Inc. (MU) 0.56% Information Technology 0.15 0.18 0.20
Sea Limited (SE) 0.30% Consumer Discretionary 0.36 0.13 0.74
Brinker International, Inc. (EAT) 0.22% Consumer Discretionary 0.38 0.11 0.42
Agnico Eagle Mines Limited (AEM) 0.26% Materials 0.41 0.32 0.31
First Solar, Inc. (FSLR) 0.31% Information Technology 0.43 0.22 0.31

Ratios are calculated by the author using data from Seeking Alpha and the ETFs

Final Thoughts

In sum, FFLG's stock selection and weighting, with a lot of emphasis put on NVDA, secured its GARP edge over SCHG, which could have been used as a nice basis for a bullish thesis. However, its performance is a bit disappointing: it was capable of capitalizing on the AI narrative and the interest rate cuts but not to the extent sufficient to secure outperformance versus SCHG and FELG. And given the $529.3 million AUM, its liquidity is healthy but not as strong as SCHG's. So I believe the Hold rating should be maintained.

data.title: Apple: Despite The Selloff, 2026 Still Priced At A Premium
data.description:
It's Technically Oversold- Is It a Buy?

In September 2025, I downgraded Apple Inc.'s (AAPL) to Hold from Buy, citing that over-exuberance on tariff relief and AI initiatives had pushed its valuation higher. Since then, the stock has traded flat, only up 2%. AAPL recently suffered from a 10% pullback, which triggered an oversold condition. So, is it a buy now?

I think a near-term mild rebound is possible, but AAPL's valuation is still unattractive, given its mid-single-digit product sales and muted margin expansion. Although the company is expected to report strong 1Q FY2026 earnings, I believe the AI upgrade cycle for iPhone won't be sustainable once users already have a device with Apple Intelligence.

Given the forward EPS consensus, the stock is trading at 31.4x non-GAAP P/E fwd, which doesn't show plenty of upside to me. Among the Magnificent 7 names, such as Microsoft (MSFT), Google (GOOGL), and Amazon (AMZN), all are trading at +30x forward P/E, but their growth is much stronger than AAPL. Their revenue growth has been maintained in the double digits with strong margin expansion. Particularly, we saw recent news saying that GOOGL's market cap surpassed AAPL for the first time since 2019. While AAPL is not a hyperscaler and has a different business model, trading at a similar valuation implies that the stock has already reflected much good news. Unless you want to take advantage of a short-lived technical bounce after the oversold condition, I still maintain my neutral view on AAPL.

Can We See an Inflection in FY2026?

Let's do a quick recap. We haven't seen an all-clear inflection in FY2025. How about FY2026? As shown on the tables, AAPL's top-line growth was largely driven by the Americas region, where growth accelerated to 6.8% in FY2025 vs. 2.8% YoY in FY2024. We can see Apple Intelligence helped push iPhone sales higher. Since iPhone 16 integrated with AI across all models for the first time, we could anticipate many users probably planned to upgrade.

Even so, China's iPhone sales remained sluggish in FY2025. The revenue in China has declined for two consecutive years. While the Americas region has already performed well in 2025, I believe upside surprises will matter more if the China region can rebound in 2026 amidst increasing competition in the Chinese smartphone market.

After the release of iPhone 17 in September last year, according to Counterpoint Research, it was encouraging to see a 37% monthly jump in China sales, pushing iPhone market share to 25% in the region. Management previously noted a return to growth in China in 1Q FY2026, and I believe China's revenue could come in ahead of expectations this time. They previously guided a double-digit growth in iPhone sales in 1Q FY2026. They even noted that "it will be our best quarter ever." However, the question is what catalysts can maintain that momentum beyond 1Q.

Nevertheless, the stock had rallied nearly 20% from September to December, which I believe fully priced in this optimism. Despite the recent selloff, it's still 10% above the pre-sale levels. Therefore, I would see limited upside from here after 1Q FY2026 earnings.

Fast AI Upgrade Cycle May Not Last Long

While we've seen strong iPhone sales, I doubt it would be sustainable, and it's likely seeing a growth normalization soon, as most iPhone users already owned an iPhone 16 or 17. I acknowledge that iPhone 17 has better performance with the A19 chip, but there isn't any new "game changing" feature that would force users to upgrade again, especially if they already have an iPhone 16 that supports Apple Intelligence. Unless new models significantly differentiate from the old ones (like 15 vs. 16), I don't think regular consumers will upgrade their phones on an annual basis.

Most importantly, iPhone users can access Apple Intelligence and some AI features for free (at least for now). If the company does not come up with subscription-based tiers for its AI services (like ChatGPT and Gemini), AI monetization could remain slow, which will not drive a strong growth inflection.

Earnings Growth Is Strong, but FCF Declines

AAPL's GAAP EPS came in at $7.46 in FY2025, showing an impressive 22.7% YoY growth. However, EPS was flat in FY2024 and FY2023; we still need a few more quarters to see if the growth will sustain. Based on Seeking Alpha consensus, AAPL is expected to grow EPS roughly 10.6% YoY in FY2026. If we consider next year's EPS consensus, its GAAP EPS CAGR from FY2022 to FY2026 would be 7.8%, significantly below other mega caps that have generated 20% to 50% growth during the same period.

In addition, AAPL's FCF declined 9.2% YoY in FY2025, driven by lower CFO (impacted by a one-time income tax charge of $10.2 billion, net) and higher capex, as indicated on the table above. Excluding the income tax impact, AAPL's FCF would be flat YoY.

Premium Valuation Has Priced in Much of Growth Optimism

AAPL is currently trading at 34.8x GAAP P/E TTM, about 13% higher than its 5-year average. Considering non-GAAP EPS consensus (FTM), the multiple comes down to 31.4x. But it still doesn't show a bargain.

The company's top-line growth has been sluggish, hovering around the mid-single digits (2% YoY growth in FY2024 and 6.4% YoY growth in FY2025). This is significantly below the double-digit growth in MSFT, GOOGL, and AMZN. While AAPL's earnings growth has started to accelerate, which is a positive, the sustainability of earnings growth is a key factor to justify a higher valuation. Given the stock is already at +30x forward P/E, I would say much of the optimism has been priced in. Despite the recent selloff, the stock doesn't look that cheap. Therefore, AAPL remains a Hold to me.

Conclusion

In summary, AAPL is expected to deliver a strong 1Q FY2026 earnings in the coming weeks, supported by double-digit revenue growth and a potential sales rebound in China. However, the stock already had a strong rally after the iPhone 17 debut. Despite the recent pullback, it still trades at +30x forward P/E (though the stock is technically oversold). I also don't believe the AI upgrade cycle will last long. Therefore, without better AI monetization strategies (such as AI subscription tiers), double-digit revenue growth won't be sustainable. Lastly, we also don't know if the global trade tension will escalate again in 2026. For that reason, the stock looks fairly valued given its premium multiple vs. future growth outlook.

data.title: Mar Vista U.S. Quality Q4 2025 Portfolio Update
data.description:
The following segment was excerpted from the Mar Vista U.S. Quality Q4 2025 Commentary.

During the quarter, we initiated new investments in Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company (TSM) and Netflix, Inc. (NFLX). We also exited our positions in Equifax Inc. (EFX), Roper Technologies (ROP), and Magnum Ice Cream Company N.V. (MICC). We added to our holdings in Amazon.com Inc. (AMZN), Linde plc (LIN), and Visa Inc. (V) and trimmed Amphenol Corp. (APH), Oracle Corp. (ORCL), SAP SE (SAP), and Salesforce Inc. (CRM).

Taiwan Semiconductor (TSM) is the dominant manufacturer of semiconductors for the world’s leading fabless chip designers including NVIDIA, Apple, Broadcom, and others. TSM is the world’s preeminent semiconductor foundry due to its leadership position in producing the most advanced chips that power the world’s most sophisticated supercomputers, personal computers, and mobile devices. It is also one of only two manufacturers that operates at the leading edge of semiconductor process technology. This technological leadership, reinforced by scale economies, proprietary process know-how, and decades of accumulated manufacturing expertise, creates a formidable competitive moat that is increasingly difficult for other foundries to replicate. As a result, TSM earns structurally higher gross margins than its peers through a combination of technology-driven pricing power and superior manufacturing efficiency.

TSM has also benefited from ongoing consolidation within the semiconductor industry, which has accelerated demand for integrated systems built on the most advanced process nodes. At the same time, the rapid growth of artificial intelligence, high-performance computing, and the Internet of Things is driving sustained demand for leading-edge semiconductors, as computing becomes increasingly intelligent and interconnected. We believe the global arms race to develop artificial general intelligence will support multiple years of robust growth for foundries with true leading-edge capabilities. TSM is uniquely positioned to benefit regardless of which fabless chip architecture ultimately dominates high-performance computing, as its customers span the full spectrum of leading AI, CPU, GPU, and custom accelerator designs.

Semiconductor manufacturing is inherently cyclical and capital intensive, yet we believe these factors further strengthen TSM’s moat rather than weaken it. The escalating cost and complexity of advanced-node fabrication requires tens of billions of dollars in sustained annual capital expenditures, which raises the barriers to entry and constrains credible competition. During industry downturns, TSM’s scale, balance sheet strength, and customer entrenchment should allow it to continue investing through the cycle, widening the technology gap versus smaller or less well-capitalized peers. We believe these structural advantages should enable TSM to generate returns on invested capital well above its cost of capital across the business cycle and to compound intrinsic value at rates meaningfully in excess of broader market growth over our investment horizon.

Netflix (NFLX) has built a durable economic moat around its vertically-integrated, globally-scaled streaming business. As the first company to establish a global subscription media platform within the $500 billion TV market, Netflix is now reaping the benefits of its early leadership. Its march toward global dominance has been propelled by substantial investments in both technology and content. The broader media industry, meanwhile, is undergoing a structural transformation driven by technology, as the traditional TV bundle fades and legacy media companies scramble to replicate Netflix’s success. However, without global scale, the escalating costs of competing in the content wars are unsustainable. With more than 300 million members, Netflix enjoys the lowest content cost per subscriber in the industry, an advantage that enables it to profitably outspend rivals and accelerate its competitive flywheel. We expect this edge to continue compounding over time.

At the time of purchase, Netflix’s stock was down nearly 20% from its February peak. Near-term macroeconomic uncertainty likely contributed to this volatility, creating an attractive entry opportunity. This downward pressure was further intensified by market skepticism surrounding Netflix’s $82.7 billion bid for Warner Bros. Discovery. While the deal promises a massive expansion of premium intellectual property (IP), investors have reacted with caution to the prospect of a bidding war with Paramount Skydance and the operational complexities of such a historic pivot.

While we acknowledge these integration risks, we believe the long-term upside significantly outweighs the near-term downside. In our view, the acquisition of iconic franchises like Harry Potter, the DC Universe, and the HBO library represents a generational shift from a "streaming platform" to a "global media powerhouse." We believe the market is over-discounting the strategic moat this IP provides, creating a compelling risk-reward profile for patient investors.

With industry-leading profitability, Netflix continues to pull ahead of competitors through disciplined execution, a high-quality content slate, and unmatched scale. Additionally, we believe the company’s ad-supported tier and push into live sports represent significant new growth initiatives. Entering these catalysts from a position of strength—as the global leader in streaming revenue, subscribers, viewing hours, content, and profits—positions Netflix for intrinsic value compounding of approximately 15–20% over our investment horizon.

We divested our position in Equifax (EFX) during the quarter following a strategic shift by FICO, a leading provider of credit scores to the mortgage industry. FICO announced plans to sell its credit scores directly to mortgage underwriters, bypassing the credit bureaus and thereby pressuring the economics that EFX has historically captured in the credit-scoring value chain. This change increases uncertainty around the long-term distribution of economics within the mortgage credit ecosystem and widens the range of potential outcomes for EFX. Coupled with a slower-than-expected recovery in the housing market, we believe this evolving industry dynamic has weakened the risk-reward profile of the investment. As a result, we chose to exit the position and redeploy capital into opportunities within the portfolio that offer more attractive return potential with greater visibility.

After a decade-long partnership with Roper Technologies (ROP), we have made a strategic decision to exit our position. Roper has an exceptional track record of compounding capital. However, our decision to sell is a reflection of our commitment to maintaining a portfolio of high-conviction, high-growth businesses.

Our decision to sell was based on three factors. Firstly, Roper’s organic growth rates have begun to lag its pure-play software peers. Roper's diversified model now acts as a drag when compared to specialized software-as-a-service companies that can focus a majority of their R&D on a single, high-growth vertical. Secondly, Roper's "niche market leader" strategy is built on acquiring businesses with high barriers to entry and strong recurring revenue. However, we believe many of these businesses are approaching market saturation, which limits their future growth prospects. Lastly, the valuation no longer provides an attractive margin of safety given the first two challenges. At a forward P/E ratio often exceeding that of the market and closer to higher growth peers, the market is pricing in a level of growth we believe is optimistic given the underlying organic trends.

We also decided to divest our minor holding in the Magnum Ice Cream Company (MICC) following its spin-off from Unilever, as the company fails to meet our core intrinsic value growth requirements. While Magnum remains a global leader in the premium ice cream category, its projected organic revenue growth of approximately 3% falls short of our mandate for high-compounding business models.

The U.S. Quality Composite was created 12/01/07, with an inception date of 12/31/03. On 12/01/2024 the name of the composite changed from Strategic Growth to U.S. Quality. All returns are based in U.S. dollars and are computed using a time-weighted total rate of return. The composite is defined to include all fully discretionary portfolios with no minimum or maximum account value, managed in accordance with Mar Vista’s U.S. Quality strategy, and that paid for execution on a transaction basis. data.title: Mar Vista U.S. Quality Q4 2025 Top Contributors And Detractors
data.description:
The following segment was excerpted from the Mar Vista U.S. Quality Q4 2025 Commentary.

Mar Vista’s U.S. Quality strategy returned +0.20% net-of-fees in the fourth quarter of 2025. The Russell 1000® Index and the S&P 500® Index returned +2.41% and +2.65%, respectively. Stock selection within communication services, consumer discretionary and financials positively impacted performance, while stocks in information technology, materials, and healthcare detracted from performance during the quarter.

Alphabet Inc. (GOOG), Johnson & Johnson (JNJ), and Danaher Corp. (DHR) were among the portfolio’s top contributors for the quarter, appreciating +28.93%, +12.31%, and +15.62%, respectively. Alternatively, Oracle Corp. (ORCL), Microsoft Corp. (MSFT), and Linde plc (LIN) detracted from performance, declining -30.58%, -6.45%, and -9.90%, respectively.

Alphabet’s (GOOG) Ǫ4 performance marks a significant triumph, characterized by a rare “beat and raise” narrative across all critical business segments. The company’s recent earnings report was driven by a balanced contribution from its legacy Search and YouTube divisions, with Google Cloud emerging as the standout performer. This remarkable transformation has propelled Cloud from a margin drag to a high-octane profit center. Operational leverage indicates that Alphabet is successfully scaling its infrastructure while maintaining disciplined cost controls.

Strategically, the “AI-first” pivot has transcended its initial roadmap status and become a tangible driver of user engagement and enterprise spending. The milestone of 650 million monthly active users on the Gemini App serves as a tangible counter-narrative to concerns about AI-driven search disruption. By integrating Gemini across its entire ecosystem, Alphabet has fortified its competitive advantage, enabling it to defend its search franchise while simultaneously capturing new market share in the enterprise cloud sector. This fundamental strength underpins a trajectory of high-teens EPS growth, making a 20x–25x earnings multiple appear conservative despite the stock’s recent appreciation.

The company's consistent execution across key business segments and positive market sentiment, despite a significant headwind from Stelara generics, contributed to its outperformance relative to broader market indices. Management continues to expect 5-7% revenue growth through 2030 which exceeds consensus estimates.

After lagging through the first three quarters of 2025, Danaher’s (DHR) stock rebounded during Ǫ4 as bioprocessing, life science, and diagnostics demand continued to recover from a cyclical trough. On the 3Ǫ25 call, management established conservative 2026 growth expectations. Revenue is expected to continue to lag long-term trends at 3-6% but improve throughout the year. Operating leverage should drive high single digit EPS growth. The company’s strong balance sheet and capital deployment strategy should provide accretive growth for the foreseeable future.

Investor enthusiasm for Oracle’s (ORCL) stock in calendar year 2025 was initially driven by several multi-billion-dollar contracts it signed with leading AI companies, including OpenAI and Meta. However, in Ǫ4 sentiment for ORCL’s growth prospects shifted to skepticism, as investors began to scrutinize the return profile of the substantial capital investments required to support the approximately $500 billion of contracts signed by Oracle.

As a result, investors grew increasingly concerned about Oracle’s ability to fund these investments and the concentration of future revenue exposure tied to OpenAI. While OpenAI is an early leader in artificial intelligence with nearly 900 million weekly average users, it remains relatively unproven given the scale and duration of its contractual commitments. We recognize the risks associated with OpenAI’s ability to fund these obligations and to successfully monetize its platform beyond consumer subscriptions, including through advertising and deeper penetration of the enterprise IT market.

Given the widening range of potential outcomes associated with Oracle’s elevated capital needs, we reduced our position in ORCL during Ǫ4. We continue to maintain a smaller weighting, as we believe Oracle is capable of managing its capital requirements and generating attractive long-term returns from these investments.

Microsoft’s (MSFT) stock came under pressure in Ǫ4 as investors grew concerned about the rising cost of building AI data infrastructure to support foundation models such as OpenAI. Capital expenditures across Microsoft and its hyperscaler peers accelerated in calendar 2025 and are expected to increase again in 2026. While these investments are substantial, Microsoft is well positioned to fund this growth through its large and expanding base of operating cash flows. That said, the company does have meaningful exposure to OpenAI, which remains relatively unproven given the scale and duration of its contractual commitments.

We continue to maintain Microsoft as a top holding, as we believe the company’s financial strength, diversified revenue streams, and broad customer base provide significant resilience. Microsoft is seeing accelerating growth within Azure, its hyperscale cloud platform which remains capacity-constrained, alongside increasing adoption of its Copilot offerings across its extensive enterprise customer base. We believe Microsoft should be well positioned to generate attractive long-term returns from its partnership with OpenAI and to effectively monetize generative AI capabilities across its global enterprise IT footprint through its expanding suite of Copilot and AI-enabled products.

While the company remains a high-quality global leader in industrial gases, shares of Linde plc (LIN) declined nearly 10% in Ǫ4 due to a persistent industrial gas volume recession, softer guidance and global macroeconomic concerns. From a macro standpoint, the company continues to struggle with negative base volumes in its core industrial segments. Much of this is attributable to economic stagnation in Europe and suppressed demand in China. While price increases helped offset some costs, they were insufficient to mask the 1-3% volume declines evidenced in these key territories.

In response to this weakness, management announced cautious guidance in late October and during subsequent updates in December, causing consensus earnings estimate reductions. These revisions drove P/E multiple contraction as investors rotated out of high-quality, defensive names like Linde into higher growth opportunities in other sectors like technology.

Despite the recent dip, we believe much of Linde's woes are transitory in nature and not a breakdown of the business model. The company's $10+ billion backlog, particularly in clean hydrogen and electronics, provides a strong foundation for a long-term growth recovery. We remain holders of the shares of this high-quality, wide-moat business in anticipation of a future growth reacceleration.

Market leadership expanded modestly during the quarter, with increased participation across various sectors and asset classes beyond the mega-cap complex. Commodities experienced significant gains, and market internals started reflecting a shift away from a rally solely driven by liquidity and AI infrastructure development toward one increasingly influenced by earnings strength and fundamental differentiation. These evolving dynamics suggest that the setup for 2026 may be significantly different from the previous three years.

Late in the year, policy developments added further complexity. The federal government shutdown created a temporary data vacuum, delaying economic releases and complicating both investor interpretation and the Federal Reserve’s reaction function. This uncertainty compounded the unsettled tariff paths and ongoing trade negotiations, which continued to disrupt corporate planning cycles and heightened market sensitivity to headline news. While earnings strength ultimately propelled equities higher, these policy crosscurrents reinforced the uneven nature of this year’s appreciation and contributed to increased underlying volatility as the economic cycle progressed into its later stages.

Portfolio Activity

During the quarter, we initiated new investments in Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company (TSM) and Netflix, Inc. (NFLX). We also exited our positions in Equifax Inc. (EFX), Roper Technologies (ROP), and Magnum Ice Cream Company N.V. (MICC). We added to our holdings in Amazon.com Inc. (AMZN), Linde plc (LIN), and Visa Inc. (V) and trimmed Amphenol Corp. (APH), Oracle Corp. (ORCL), SAP SE (SAP), and Salesforce Inc. (CRM).

Taiwan Semiconductor (TSM) is the dominant manufacturer of semiconductors for the world’s leading fabless chip designers including NVIDIA, Apple, Broadcom, and others. TSM is the world’s preeminent semiconductor foundry due to its leadership position in producing the most advanced chips that power the world’s most sophisticated supercomputers, personal computers, and mobile devices. It is also one of only two manufacturers that operates at the leading edge of semiconductor process technology. This technological leadership, reinforced by scale economies, proprietary process know-how, and decades of accumulated manufacturing expertise, creates a formidable competitive moat that is increasingly difficult for other foundries to replicate. As a result, TSM earns structurally higher gross margins than its peers through a combination of technology-driven pricing power and superior manufacturing efficiency.

TSM has also benefited from ongoing consolidation within the semiconductor industry, which has accelerated demand for integrated systems built on the most advanced process nodes. At the same time, the rapid growth of artificial intelligence, high-performance computing, and the Internet of Things is driving sustained demand for leading-edge semiconductors, as computing becomes increasingly intelligent and interconnected. We believe the global arms race to develop artificial general intelligence will support multiple years of robust growth for foundries with true leading-edge capabilities. TSM is uniquely positioned to benefit regardless of which fabless chip architecture ultimately dominates high-performance computing, as its customers span the full spectrum of leading AI, CPU, GPU, and custom accelerator designs.

Semiconductor manufacturing is inherently cyclical and capital intensive, yet we believe these factors further strengthen TSM’s moat rather than weaken it. The escalating cost and complexity of advanced-node fabrication requires tens of billions of dollars in sustained annual capital expenditures, which raises the barriers to entry and constrains credible competition. During industry downturns, TSM’s scale, balance sheet strength, and customer entrenchment should allow it to continue investing through the cycle, widening the technology gap versus smaller or less well-capitalized peers. We believe these structural advantages should enable TSM to generate returns on invested capital well above its cost of capital across the business cycle and to compound intrinsic value at rates meaningfully in excess of broader market growth over our investment horizon.

Netflix (NFLX) has built a durable economic moat around its vertically-integrated, globally-scaled streaming business. As the first company to establish a global subscription media platform within the $500 billion TV market, Netflix is now reaping the benefits of its early leadership. Its march toward global dominance has been propelled by substantial investments in both technology and content. The broader media industry, meanwhile, is undergoing a structural transformation driven by technology, as the traditional TV bundle fades and legacy media companies scramble to replicate Netflix’s success. However, without global scale, the escalating costs of competing in the content wars are unsustainable. With more than 300 million members, Netflix enjoys the lowest content cost per subscriber in the industry, an advantage that enables it to profitably outspend rivals and accelerate its competitive flywheel. We expect this edge to continue compounding over time.

At the time of purchase, Netflix’s stock was down nearly 20% from its February peak. Near-term macroeconomic uncertainty likely contributed to this volatility, creating an attractive entry opportunity. This downward pressure was further intensified by market skepticism surrounding Netflix’s $82.7 billion bid for Warner Bros. Discovery. While the deal promises a massive expansion of premium intellectual property (IP), investors have reacted with caution to the prospect of a bidding war with Paramount Skydance and the operational complexities of such a historic pivot.

While we acknowledge these integration risks, we believe the long-term upside significantly outweighs the near-term downside. In our view, the acquisition of iconic franchises like Harry Potter, the DC Universe, and the HBO library represents a generational shift from a "streaming platform" to a "global media powerhouse." We believe the market is over-discounting the strategic moat this IP provides, creating a compelling risk-reward profile for patient investors.

With industry-leading profitability, Netflix continues to pull ahead of competitors through disciplined execution, a high-quality content slate, and unmatched scale. Additionally, we believe the company’s ad-supported tier and push into live sports represent significant new growth initiatives. Entering these catalysts from a position of strength—as the global leader in streaming revenue, subscribers, viewing hours, content, and profits—positions Netflix for intrinsic value compounding of approximately 15–20% over our investment horizon.

We divested our position in Equifax (EFX) during the quarter following a strategic shift by FICO, a leading provider of credit scores to the mortgage industry. FICO announced plans to sell its credit scores directly to mortgage underwriters, bypassing the credit bureaus and thereby pressuring the economics that EFX has historically captured in the credit-scoring value chain. This change increases uncertainty around the long-term distribution of economics within the mortgage credit ecosystem and widens the range of potential outcomes for EFX. Coupled with a slower-than-expected recovery in the housing market, we believe this evolving industry dynamic has weakened the risk-reward profile of the investment. As a result, we chose to exit the position and redeploy capital into opportunities within the portfolio that offer more attractive return potential with greater visibility.

After a decade-long partnership with Roper Technologies (ROP), we have made a strategic decision to exit our position. Roper has an exceptional track record of compounding capital. However, our decision to sell is a reflection of our commitment to maintaining a portfolio of high-conviction, high-growth businesses.

Our decision to sell was based on three factors. Firstly, Roper’s organic growth rates have begun to lag its pure-play software peers. Roper's diversified model now acts as a drag when compared to specialized software-as-a-service companies that can focus a majority of their R&D on a single, high-growth vertical. Secondly, Roper's "niche market leader" strategy is built on acquiring businesses with high barriers to entry and strong recurring revenue. However, we believe many of these businesses are approaching market saturation, which limits their future growth prospects. Lastly, the valuation no longer provides an attractive margin of safety given the first two challenges. At a forward P/E ratio often exceeding that of the market and closer to higher growth peers, the market is pricing in a level of growth we believe is optimistic given the underlying organic trends.

We also decided to divest our minor holding in the Magnum Ice Cream Company (MICC) following its spin-off from Unilever, as the company fails to meet our core intrinsic value growth requirements. While Magnum remains a global leader in the premium ice cream category, its projected organic revenue growth of approximately 3% falls short of our mandate for high-compounding business models.

Outlook

Looking ahead, the path for markets in 2026 rests on a delicate balance between supportive fundamentals and rising economic risks. The Fed’s easing cycle and resilient corporate earnings are still constructive, but the market’s narrow leadership and elevated valuations, especially among AI-linked mega-caps, leave equities vulnerable to sentiment reversals. Policy uncertainty, including trade negotiations and the recent shutdown-related data disruption, adds another layer of volatility.

Within this environment, we believe the structural shift driven by Artificial Intelligence, particularly the acceleration of Generative AI (GenAI), is now transitioning from proof-of-concept to demonstrable return on investment. Early monetization has been most visible in three areas where we have meaningful exposure: advertising, through platforms such as Alphabet and Meta; cloud computing, via holdings including Microsoft, Amazon, Alphabet, and Oracle; and semiconductors and connectivity, where NVIDIA, Broadcom, and Amphenol continue to benefit from exceptional demand visibility and strong pricing power.

Across these categories, companies deploying AI infrastructure are beginning to see tangible improvements in ROIC, driven by more efficient ad targeting, premium AI cloud services with high utilization, and multi-year contracts for high-performance compute and networking components. AI is becoming a true productivity enhancer, with capital equipment substituting for or augmenting human labor, yet markets remain sensitive to the heavy upfront capex required. As spending ramps further, we expect periods of volatility as investors digest the timing mismatch between cost recognition and long-run monetization. Our strategy is to capture AI’s powerful optionality while maintaining valuation discipline around businesses whose fundamentals are still robust, independent of the AI cycle.

We believe the transition from infrastructure build-out to enterprise and consumer monetization will accelerate into 2026. While the size and pace of required capex may still produce episodic volatility, the underlying demand for AI capabilities remains durable and economically justified. By maintaining diversified exposure across the AI value chain, paired with our long-standing emphasis on strong competitive advantages, intrinsic value growth, and disciplined capital allocation, we aim to capture the long-term compounding potential of this technological shift while staying true to the principles of high-quality investing.

Against this backdrop, we believe fundamentals must increasingly take center stage. After a strong multi-year rerating, valuation expansion has already played a significant role, and 2026 equity returns are likely to be more influenced by earnings and cash-flow growth rather than higher multiples. This shift places a premium on businesses with enduring competitive advantages, recurring revenue streams, strong balance sheets, and high returns on incremental capital.

For U.S. Quality, this environment highlights the value of a disciplined, valuation-aware approach. We continue to emphasize companies with long runways for intrinsic value compounding while using volatility to rebalance away from crowded exposures and toward under-appreciated drivers of durable growth. Elevated concentration, policy uncertainty, and macro signals all reinforce the importance of owning businesses that can control their destiny through superior economics, strategic clarity, and consistent cash-flow generation. We remain agile, balancing innovation-driven insights with strict risk management, to ensure the portfolio is positioned for sustainable, long-term growth regardless of shifting external headwinds.

data.title: SRV: This 19% Yielder Is Far From Sustainable
data.description:
Back in October, 2024, I issued an article on NXG Cushing Midstream Energy Fund (SRV), which is a closed end fund focusing on the midstream space. As a midstream bull, when I noticed that the yield offered by midstream-based SRV was much higher than what we could get from the pure-play holdings, I instantly gave it a try - i.e., did a deep-dive assessment of whether the case is really a buy.

Even though I liked the underlying exposures, the unfavorable leverage structure coupled with a significant reliance on NAV sales was what held me back from deploying capital in SRV.

Since then, SRV's share price has contracted by ~11%, but on a total return basis, the CEF is up by a bit less than 10%. The ride for SRV has been quite volatile; see the chart below:

In the meantime, the midstream index has delivered both less volatile and stronger (alpha of 4%) performance compared to SRV.

However, what we have now is SRV, which is yielding almost 19%, which looks very attractive, to say the least.

Let's take a quick look at whether SRV has become a more justified buy given the elevated yield, lower base rates environment, and overall consistently strong distributions, which have not been cut since 2015, when we experienced a significant oil price plunge on a global scale.

Thesis review

First of all, we have to start with the yield aspect, which is the key value prop area that makes SRV so enticing, at least optically.

It is true that on both a TTM and FWD basis, the yield is close to 20%. For any energy- or energy infrastructure related security, this is very, very solid. In fact, I doubt that we would find a comparable CEF out there that offers this kind of income.

Yet, one of the main drivers for the elevated yield statistic is the huge special distribution, which took place right before we entered 2026 - $2.14 per unit compared to a monthly stream of $0.45 per unit:

The thing is that this is the first time SRV has made such a big payout going all the way back to 2007, when the first distribution started. Plus, the distribution was exclusively funded by the realization of long-term capital gain, not the collected income from the underlying portfolio holdings.

If we adjust for this one-off and base the yield calculus on the current monthly income streams, then we arrive at a 13.5% yield, which is still high, but nowhere close to the ~20% mark.

The next question is whether the 13.5% looks solid and sustainable enough for truly durable income investors to consider integrating it into their portfolios. The answer is not that straightforward.

There are two sides to the story.

On the one hand, SRV has deployed capital into a diversified base of energy companies (power, downstream, upstream, midstream etc.), which certainly reduces the overall risk in the Fund. For example, the AI-induced tailwinds for power utilities and energy infrastructure offset the headwinds from lower oil prices and increased political risk (pressure) on the renewables.

The Top 10 holdings consist mostly of investment grade businesses, which introduce another layer of safety.

Then SRV has sourced external leverage that currently sponsors 25.95% of the total assets. And it is positive to see that all of the borrowings here stem from short-term debt funding sources, which is exactly where a Fund wants to be given the falling base rate environment, while the long-term yields remain somewhat elevated. Namely, the lower short-term rates allow SRV to reduce the interest expense item, thereby supporting the net interest income generation, which at the end of the day is the main source for sustainable income distributions.

On the other hand, there are a couple of items, which might argue against SRV. My main concern is the type of fuel that SRV utilized for making the distributions happen. The distributions made over the past couple of months have been entirely funded by the return of capital. What it means is that a major chunk of the current income streams that we get from SRV is simply the capital that we have invested when opening a position in SRV (i.e., we are getting back our invested principal). Also, the annualized distribution of NAV greatly exceeds what SRV effectively generates on NAV - i.e., 14.05% vs. 7.07%.

The NAV dynamic chart confirms that:

Moreover, above I mentioned that the defensive portfolio allocation choice by SRV serves as a shield for energy-related risks and, in the long run, should smooth out the value profile of the Fund (the beauty of diversification).

However, this comes at the expense of the underlying core yield level. As I said, many of SRV's holdings carry robust balance sheets and have investment-grade credit ratings, which inherently tend to push down the yields (due to balanced risks). So, to fund the juicy distributions and close the gap between portfolio core yield and SRV total yield, the CEF has to rely on leverage and capital gains. The former is there, but the latter is, per definition, volatile and unpredictable. Some years it might work, and some might not.

Finally, I have to finish with the leverage part of the equation. The current interest expense that is incurred by SRV is 2.38%, which is roughly 130% below the SOFR. And this difference is just from SOFR; I am not talking about credit risk premiums, which are low but still there. So, what I am saying here is that as we go forward, unless there are additional interest rate cuts, SRV is likely to experience headwinds on the NII generation side from more expensive debt service.

The bottom line

In my humble opinion, I would say that we can forget about accessing close to 20% yield from SRV in a sustainable fashion. Also, we have to be aware that a notable part of income we get from SRV comes in the form of ROC.

Yet, does this mean that SRV is uninvestable?

The answer is it depends.

I see the merit of opening an exposure to SRV if investors want to capture truly high-yielding passive income streams in a monthly fashion, do not want to deal with K-1 forms, and seek a diversified energy position.

Personally, I am not holding any stake in SRV and instead prefer to focus on high-yielding (generally, 8%+) pure-play energy businesses that fund distributions from pure cash generation instead of capital gains harvesting or ROC.

data.title: HubSpot: Buying The 'Death Of Software' Narrative Hand Over Fist (Upgrade)
data.description:
I have been looking deeply in the software sector amidst surging fears that generative AI may disrupt the space. HubSpot (HUBS) is a former growth darling, but its premium multiple has all but vanished. Amidst the chaos, the company continues to post solid top-line growth alongside significant progress on profitability. The valuation has fallen to a point at which stellar returns are possible even without a return to its former glory. I am upgrading the stock to a "Strong Buy" as beaten-down software is my top overarching theme beginning the new year.

HUBS Stock Price

I last covered HUBS in 2022, where I gave it a bullish rating. The stock has massively underperformed the market since, as it has failed to live up to the overly optimistic growth trajectory.

The re-rating downward of software stocks has created great fear as well as a buying opportunity.

HUBS Stock Key Metrics

HUBS is an enterprise software company known for its customer relationship management (‘CRM’) products. Whereas Salesforce (CRM) dominates among larger enterprises, HUBS has found a successful position servicing the small-to-medium enterprise segment. Like CRM, HUBS has integrated generative AI into its product suite. That appears not to be enough to quell concerns that it might be a victim of generative AI.

In its most recent quarter, HUBS generated 21% YoY revenue growth to $810 million, crushing guidance of between $785.0 million and $787.0 million. The company generated $161 million of non-GAAP operating profits, slightly exceeding guidance of between $156.0 million and $157.0 million.

I note that the company has made steady progress on GAAP profitability, generating its first quarter of positive GAAP operating income in the quarter.

HUBS ended the quarter with $1.7 billion of cash versus no debt. The company spent $375.0 million of cash to repurchase stock in the quarter, which is quite aggressive given the $161 million in non-GAAP operating income.

Looking ahead, management guided for the company to see around 18% YoY revenue growth to $830 million, marking some deceleration from the third quarter.

On the conference call, management called generative AI a clear opportunity, in part due to the change in search behavior. With AI overviews gaining more and more market share, the company has found success in its Loop product, which helps customers “show up in AI-generated answers.” Management was directly asked if they could return to 20% growth, to which they answered that they “believe we can grow faster than where we are today, and we are focused on doing it in a durable, disciplined way.” I would not be surprised if AI agents help accelerate the growth rates at some point in the future.

Is HUBS Stock a Buy, Sell, or Hold?

At recent prices, HUBS traded at around 5.4x forward sales. Consensus estimates call for the company to sustain around 14% to 16% top-line growth for many years.

That valuation multiple represents an in-line to slight premium to CRM. HUBS previously traded at a large relative and absolute premium in the past, with its 5-year average valuation at around 13x price to sales. I do not foresee a quick return to those levels, especially with CRM trading where it is today, as its faster relative growth is arguably more than offset by CRM’s far higher GAAP margins. Even so, this is a report about HUBS and not CRM.

Management has targeted up to 22% non-GAAP operating margins by 2027 and 25% over the long term, but I view these targets to be too conservative. The company’s gross margins stand at over 80%, and I would expect operating leverage to be quite significant if and when management decides to prioritize profitability.

Based on what I saw in the aftermath of the 2022 crash in tech stocks, I would not be surprised if that focus on profitability were to happen sooner rather than later. With HUBS trading at bargain basement multiples and growth possibly set to face some headwinds, it would make sense for the company to drive more of its excitement from margin expansion. Based on 25% GAAP net margins over the long term, the stock trades at around 22x long-term earnings. That looks like a cheap multiple relative to the 16% forward top-line growth rate. I typically assume that these names can achieve at least 30% GAAP net margins over the long term. I base that on the higher margins seen at mature names like Adobe (ADBE) and Microsoft (MSFT). In the case of HUBS, this target looks reasonable, especially with the company on the cusp of sustainable GAAP operating profitability. That would place the stock at around 18x long-term earnings, which would be a hair above a 1x price-to-earnings growth ratio (‘PEG ratio’). I see HUBS re-rating to a 1.5x PEG ratio at the very least, equating to around 7.2x sales or a $500 stock price over the next 12 months. The main catalyst would be the aforementioned projected margin expansion, continued execution on its share repurchase program, and any sentiment shift with regard to its business model quality. I still view HUBS to be the kind of sticky software vendor that customers are unlikely to switch away from, and thus would not rule out the stock eventually reaching more premium valuations in the 10x to 12x sales range (equating to 33x to 40x long-term earnings). HUBS is offering considerable upside to 7.2x sales given what I view to be more than manageable risk.

The key question, of course, is whether the company is at risk due to generative AI allowing its customers to simply “vibe code” an in-house product. CTO and co-founder Dharmesh Shah made a post on X addressing this very topic, noting that they themselves have not replaced any of their enterprise software subscriptions, as it makes far more sense to buy instead of build. I agree with that take - at the end of the day, I find it highly unlikely that HUBS’s customers would be able to (or desire to) develop a competitive product and maintain it at an acceptable cost.

I find it interesting that the market, primarily non-software-focused individuals, has somehow come to the collective conclusion that generative AI will disrupt enterprise software even if we have yet to see enterprise software companies themselves vibe code replacement products in-house. It is possible that one consequence of generative AI is that it has empowered the individual in ways not seen before, but perhaps over-extrapolation of that empowerment has created the opportunity we see in HUBS today.

HUBS Stock Risks

It is possible that generative AI really does lead to disruption. Perhaps even if vibe coding does not prove to be the disruptive force that the market believes it is, CRM might be able to use generative AI to improve its competitive positioning. It is possible that management engages in expensive M&A that destroys shareholder value. It is possible that the company is unable to realize operating leverage (though this seems unlikely given the promises of efficiency from AI).

HUBS Stock Conclusion

HUBS might see growth rates move lower before they go higher, if ever, as there probably will be some potential headwinds from generative AI. That said, I think the magnitude of the potential slowdown is being priced in too aggressively to the downside. The company maintains a net cash balance sheet and looks ready to enter positive GAAP operating profitability. The stock looks too cheap here, and I expect a re-rating as management executes on margin expansion. HUBS is the kind of stock I look for in the Best of Breed Growth Stocks Portfolio. HUBS is a "Strong Buy."

data.title: Huntington Bancshares: Well-Positioned Into Q1 Earnings
data.description:
Shares of Huntington Bancshares (HBAN) have been a solid performer over the past year, gaining just under 10%. 2025 was a transformative year for the regional bank, which did two acquisitions as it has sought to scale up, expand into higher-growth markets like Texas, and deepen its exposure to higher-margin businesses like wealth management. This strategic direction, combined with its conservative management team, has made me a long-term bull on HBAN. I last covered HBAN in October, rating the stock a “Buy,” and since then, shares have gained 4%, in line with the market. With HBAN now integrating its acquisitions, now is a good time to preview Q1 earnings and see if the stock remains attractive.

HBAN Transformed via M&A in 2025

Before looking forward to Q1 earnings, it is worth taking a moment to recap what was a very eventful year in 2025. First, in July, Huntington announced it was acquiring Veritex to scale up its Texas operation. This was really a bolt-on transaction at $1.9 billion in stock. It increases the size of HBAN by about 6%. Huntington received regulatory approval and closed the deal in October. HBAN is now in the process of integrating Veritex, and its Q4 financials will be the first quarter to include a contribution from this entity.

Then, surprisingly, in October, Huntington announced a second deal, accelerating its growth ambitions, with the purchase of Cadence Bank (CADE). This purchase is consistent with Huntington’s strategy to expand in the South with a significant presence in Texas and Mississippi as well as presences in other key Sun Belt metros, like Nashville, Atlanta, Tampa, and Orlando. With this deal, HBAN will operate in 21 states and half of America’s top 25 metros. Increasingly, HBAN is turning from a regional to a super-regional bank, a strategy I am supportive of given the need to increase scale and efficiency to compete with the largest institutions.

This deal is larger, with a $7.4 billion equity price, and like Veritex, the transaction is entirely in stock. Given Cadence’s larger size, there is definitely more integration risk, though it is manageable. This deal will add about $53 billion in assets, increasing the size of HBAN by just under 25% and making it one of the ten largest banks in the nation. Still, it is a fraction of America’s largest banks that have over $2 trillion in assets, and accordingly I do not expect much antitrust risk to the completion of the deal, especially as most of Cadence’s assets are not in HBAN’s legacy Midwest markets. The deal is ultimately expected to boost HBAN earnings power by about 10%, though there will be limited in-year impact in 2026. Shareholders have approved the deal, and HBAN expects to close it on Feb. 1. As a result, Q1 earnings are likely to be messy, given integration and closing costs.

HBAN is Poised for Ongoing Growth Now

As you can see below, HBAN’s network of branches is significantly larger than it previously was. Historically, it operated in Michigan, Ohio, and other Midwest markets. Going forward, it now has a significant presence across much of the Sun Belt, giving HBAN a profile more similar to a bank like Fifth Third (FITB). These Sun Belt markets are generally faster-growing and rich in deposits and wealth management opportunities. Additionally, with many retirees being “snowbirds” who spend much or all of their time in the Southeast, this footprint makes it easier for HBAN to meet all of its clients’ needs, especially as they reach peak wealth accumulation.

As you can see below, HBAN has historically been able to grow more quickly than peers, and loan growth has been nearly 5% over the past five years. Scaling up its presence in Chicago has facilitated this growth, but this pace is all the more impressive considering HBAN has historically been focused on the slower-growth portion of the country. With the go-forward HBAN operating across more growth markets, the ability to sustain this loan growth is heightened.

HBAN is Likely to Beat Q4 Earnings

Now, turning to earnings, Huntington is scheduled to report Q4 earnings on July 22, and analysts currently expect the bank to earn $0.38 on an adjusted basis and $0.32 on a GAAP basis. I would note that closing an acquisition during a quarter, particularly in the banking sector, can create significant accounting noise as all assets and liabilities are marked to market. Given Veritex’s comparatively small size, the EPS impact should not be too meaningful, but I do see scope for volatility in GAAP EPS, though any impact will be excluded from adjusted EPS, which will be the market’s focus. HBAN expects an $8 million impact from Veritex purchase accounting.

Huntington has a strong record of beating consensus, and it has not missed earnings since 2021. The company has also effectively talked down consensus over appearances at several conferences in late Q4, and with 14 analyst cuts over the past 90 days, HBAN has been able to set the consensus more conservative, which should make it easier to report an earnings beat next week.

Speaking at a Goldman Sachs conference in December, Huntington provided an intra-quarter update. As you can see below, growth continued to be solid in Q4. Loans were up nearly $3 billion through two months, while deposits were also up $1.3 billion. Oftentimes, loan growth slows in the latter part of December (given vacations around the holidays), while cash can seasonally build, boosting deposits. As such, I would not be surprised to see these growth rates converge a bit when actual results are announced.

In fact, given the strong performance QTD, HBAN provided insight as to how results are tracking relative to guidance. Both loans and deposits are tracking at the high end of the range, and as a result, net interest income should come in stronger than expected. Critically, with the focus on loan quality having risen amid challenges in private credit, the company still sees net charge-offs at the midpoint of its range at a very healthy mid-20bp level. All of this suggests a modest beat; I expect HBAN to earn $0.38-$0.42 when it reports Q4 results.

HBAN Has Further Upside; Shares Are a "Buy"

Alongside results, there will be significant focus on the company’s outlook as well as its capital allocation policy. Announcing two deals in ~three months is fairly aggressive, and I want to see HBAN focus primarily on integrating Cadence and continuing to grow organically. Investors will want to be sure that it is not doing too much too quickly. To that end, in December, management signaled there was no interest in further expanding its geographic footprint, and any deals would be in its existing territory to build more scale.

I expect HBAN to spend the next six months integrating Cadence and building capital to support organic growth. I am targeting mid-single-digit deposit and loan growth in 2026 with charge-offs that should be similar to 2025. As a result, I am targeting about $1.67-$1.77 of 2026 earnings. This assumes no share repurchases, as I expect HBAN to retain earnings to support organic growth, though investors have a secure 3.4% dividend yield. Absent M&A, I see scope for buybacks in 2027, but I would not be surprised to see HBAN do more bolt-on dealmaking before year-end and delay buybacks.

I view Huntington Bancshares as a secular winner within the regional banking sector. It has enjoyed solid organic growth while maintaining strong loan quality, enabling consistent earnings beats. In 2025, the company undertook significant M&A to transform its footprint, and we should start to see the benefits this year, though I expect even more benefits to appear in 2027 as it completes integration work and focuses on accelerating growth in new, attractive Sun Belt markets. I view 12x earnings as appropriate, given its leading growth profile, pointing to upside past $20. I view shares as a "Buy" into earnings.

data.title: Ruffer LLP Q4 2025 Investment Review
data.description:
It’s more than 30 years since I wrote my first Investment Review, delivered in paper form, courtesy of the Royal Mail, and to interested parties at a postage cost of 22p. Thirty-one years on, both the price of postage and the level of courtesy have changed considerably.

It was a forward-looking review, which is unsurprising, given that Ruffer at the time had no history – we were a business trundling slowly down the runway in a light plane on a long journey. As I approach retirement, this will be my last formal review – the pen may be silent, but the thought processes will continue. I shall continue to meet with my former colleagues and will thereby find myself ensconced in the happiest of circumstances, having the power of articulation but without any responsibilities.

The history and theory of investing is as old as the history of warfare, and one might reasonably think that all that could be said of each of them has been already articulated. The greatest ‘insight’ to become a reality in the 1980s was Jack Bogle’s. It was a simple one: if you are happy with average performance, then why not enjoy an above-average performance by investing in a low-cost index-hugging mutual fund? This simple idea – which transcended any insight as to the nature of an investment – has changed the face of the investing community. It has conquered the world by its monopoly, shielded from greedy competitors by its negligible price.

In modesty, I have to concede that an idea which now controls some 40% of the world’s equity and fixed interest markets is better than my contribution to the efficacy of investing. Ruffer Investment Management’s first glossy brochure stated that our investment strategy consisted of running risk portfolios which were designed not to lose money in any calendar year. Even accountants know that risk amplifies risk as well as reward, thus only riskless assets can claim to be free of the poison of capital reduction. And here was Ruffer, with no track record, saying you can have your cake and eat it.

The universal response: Jonathan Ruffer’s a lovely guy (I can only agree with that assessment), and what he’s offering, if achieved, would be wonderful. But something that is too good to be true is, er, well…not true. That was in 1994. In the event, it took until 2018 for our first failure to meet our objectives, although some years effectively produced nothing. The going has been more volatile since, with 2023 falling far short, but our long-term track record still puts to shame the riskless alternative of cash in the bank.

We don’t know ahead of time which opportunities are going to be the actual winners and which the losers – the gold and the fool’s gold are intertwined in an inextricable embrace.

In 2023, we found ourselves accused of not doing what we claimed to do – almost as if ‘cake and eat it’ was something that could be achieved faultlessly. Despite the head wound I felt from letting clients down that year, it was hard to escape the feeling that this way of investing has come of age. When I began, our investment disciplines were as much intuitive as rigorous. Today, they are incomparably better – so much so that I find myself (against all the evidence) lost for words. It has been a pleasure to report meeting our objectives strongly in 2025, a year which has increased the long-term track record of Ruffer.

How risky is the strategy? At one level, we concede that our aim not to lose money in any given year is a parallel claim to most professional investors’ ambition to ‘beat the indices.’ That ambition is accepted as an aspiration, and even the very best will achieve it only intermittently. The ‘beat the indices’ target has a defect in that, in the ebb and flow of performance, at certain intervals the bad investment professional will look rather good, and (of course) vice versa. So, for an investor adequately to gauge whether their decision to hire a manager was a good one or not requires a commitment of an unnervingly long duration. Ours is, by contrast, an absolute target, judged annually. We can safely be sacked much sooner than our index-related competitors.

What, then, are the characteristics of what we do? The only reason it works is because investments routinely, regularly, become either too cheap or too expensive. While only riskless commitments guarantee no loss, all of us can see that the imperfections of a real-live market allow for an imbalance of risk and reward, some favourable, some unfavourable, in specific investment opportunities. There is, of course, always an elephant in the room. We don’t know ahead of time which opportunities are going to be the actual winners, and which the losers – the gold and the fool’s gold are intertwined in an inextricable embrace. While each individual opportunity might not give up its secrets as to future performance, it is often possible to juxtapose two (or maybe more), which removes the unknowable risks and offers each-way upside. The ideal might, for instance, be a set of investments which do well if the oil price goes up, and a different set which will create outperformance if it goes down. In this simple example, the result is to create a different risk (eg political risk), which must then be considered separately. As a result, an investor in the Ruffer strategy is diversified away from market risk and left with the risk attached to our judgements of juxtaposing assets and portfolio construction. That is the skill we employ. While the strategy is simple, the execution is often complex.

What we took for wisdom 18 months ago has turned out to be so comprehensively wrong that my generation really don’t know what to think and hum the tune from 'My Fair Lady,' 'Just you wait, 'enry 'iggins, just you wait!'

Today we see a world where the thoughtless control the show and are making hay. A world where there is a generational battle going on, where the greybeards look at that world with incredulity. What we took for wisdom 18 months ago has turned out to be so comprehensively wrong that my generation really don’t know what to think, and hum the tune from ‘My Fair Lady’, ‘Just you wait, ’enry ’iggins, just you wait!’ Unlike my daughter, who is an academic neuroscientist, I haven’t quite got to understand how the brain works. But I find one quirk of the brain vividly fascinating: when humans think that some futurity is absolutely certain to happen, that thought cannot be disconnected from a second one – that the future event will happen very soon.

It is well understood that the generation now known as Boomers – those born before 1965 – have a larger share of the asset gravy than the generations which have followed. Today, to be in the top 1% of UK earners by income, your take-home pay would be around £90,000 per year; to be in the top 1% by assets, you need to own nearly £4 million, a sum which would take the 1% incomer over 44 years to accumulate if they spent not a penny in the meantime. This means that, for the first time since Magna Carta, people are not as well off as their parents, especially in the realm of housing. A successful professional, mid-forties, can see that the capital sum required to buy the house they grew up in is multiples more than they can afford, whether in cash or by borrowing. Suppose such a person has capital of £400,000, and the house they want is £1.8 million. There is no point going to a conventional fund manager who is pleased with a double-digit gain. What good is £440,000 when the purchase of a family house is required? But, if one is prepared to make an educated and well-informed foray into crypto, or AI, and achieve 25% a year, then it will have tripled in five years – and many of their contemporaries seem to have been doing this successfully for ten years or more. The only golden rule in this approach to investment: 'Never look down!' There will be sickening crashes and, if you try to analyse why, you will of course be strongly tempted to lose your nerve, and cash in. A lot of people made that ‘mistake’ in 2018, quite a lot in 2020, rather fewer on Liberation Day (6 April 2025), and today the warriors, as a cadre, will not make that ‘mistake’ again. This market will go on incredulifying the greybeards until it doesn’t. There’s a great line in JK Galbraith’s book on the 1929 crash, when he describes an eminent economist as ‘standing his ground’ before adding ‘but unfortunately the ground gave way under him.’

This moment is inevitable, but it need not be at all soon. If it is long delayed, it will represent salvation to those who put the motorised engine onto their monetary barge and achieved what was necessary. The question in my mind is what is its aftermath? If the uncertainties and dislocations can be managed, then, yes, there will be losers, many of them, and there will be many winners, too. But if it undermines the very fabric of the financial system, as was the case in 1873 and 1929, then the catalyst can be a mere water pistol and not the true weapon of war. In that case, the participants – the economy, the families going about their business, the high hopes of the young, the conservatism of the old – will find that the ground on which they depend gives way. The longer animal spirits control the market, the greater the ultimate confusion.

Past performance is not a guide to future performance. The value of the shares and the income from them can go down as well as up and you may not get back the full amount originally invested. The value of overseas investments will be influenced by the rate of exchange.
