data.title: TSPY Vs. SPYI: Why This 0DTE Fund Might Be Even Better Than SPYI
data.description:
Over the last couple of years, the competition among ETF providers to deliver high-yielding ETFs has led to numerous innovative income products. NEOS was one of the first fund providers to really change the covered call ETF space with its flexible out-of-the-money ("OTM") call selling and focus on tax-efficient distributions. As a result, funds like the Neos S&P 500 High Income ETF (SPYI) have garnered over $7B in assets under management ("AUM") - eclipsing AUM levels of formerly larger and more established funds like the Global X S&P 500 Covered Call ETF (XYLD).

But as fund providers continue to find different angles, SPYI has started to see its standing as one of, if not the best, S&P 500 covered call ETFs being challenged. The TappAlpha SPY Growth & Daily Income ETF (TSPY) is one of those new funds, which, despite its limited track record both as a fund and fund provider, has been delivering phenomenal performances that may be more preferable to SPYI's. It is a fund that offers more yield and higher S&P 500 upside capture, with its biggest drawback compared to SPYI being its weaker downside protection. For most income investors, however, this weaker downside protection is not necessarily a huge issue, especially given TSPY's higher upside potential. While I wouldn't necessarily sell SPYI for TSPY, it may make sense now to treat TSPY as more of a core covered call income holding. I still maintain a Buy rating on SPYI due to its consistent performance and rate TSPY as a Strong Buy.

SPYI's Strengths

What has made SPYI especially attractive over the last couple of years is how it has not only consistently generated 10%+ annual yields but also delivered price appreciation and positive total returns.

The fund has achieved this by selling monthly OTM SPX covered calls and, occasionally, credit spreads. Instead of selling calls at a fixed percentage strike price every month like funds such as XYLD, SPYI chooses its strike price to maximize growth in its underlying S&P 500 constituent holdings while targeting 12% of its NAV annualized in option premium earnings. SPYI's current trades at the time of writing include two call options expiring February 20, 2026. One has a strike price of 6985 and the other at 7055. For context, the S&P 500 is currently at 6966. The premiums generated represent approximately 1% of SPYI's net assets, which puts the fund on track to generate a 12% yield from option premiums.

Now, what has made SPYI even more attractive has been its focus on delivering tax-efficient returns. It is a common misconception that SPYI's distribution yields are too aggressive and it is simply returning your capital due to its large return of capital ("ROC") distributions. As indicated by SPYI's historical trades, however, nothing suggests that the fund is unable to cover distributions. What should be understood about SPYI's ROC is that it's a result of the fund's tax-loss harvesting strategies and done on purpose to create more "tax-efficient" distributions. ROC is a distribution classification where the payout is not immediately taxable and instead reduces your cost basis. You will then be taxed at capital gains rates when your cost basis reaches zero or when you sell your asset, in which your profit will be the sell price minus your adjusted cost basis. SPYI's distributions have averaged 82.04% ROC since its inception.

Part of the reason SPYI has also been tax-efficient is because it sells index options. Option premiums from index options are subject to 60% taxation as long-term capital gains and 40% as short-term rather than 100% short-term under section 1256 rules. This is much better compared to funds like the JPMorgan Equity Premium Income ETF (JEPI), which generate 100% ordinary income using swaps. Most investors are better off taking ROC or capital gains distributions tax-wise, but please do consult a professional tax advisor for your specific situation.

The Rise of TSPY

TSPY has many similar characteristics to SPYI. The fund is also anticipated to be tax-efficient like SPYI, as it sells 1256 index options and makes use of tax-loss harvesting strategies to generate ROC distributions. Just like SPYI, TSPY has also implemented the capability to be more flexible with its trading strategies and implement tools like call spreads. What makes TSPY potentially even better than SPYI, however, is it uses 0 days-to-expiry ("0DTE") options that have resulted in higher distribution payouts and upside capture. TSPY's yield has been noticeably higher, with it hovering around the 14% range compared to SPYI's 12%. But at the same time, TSPY's price returns have been comparable, and past total returns have indicated that TSPY does a better job of capturing gains in the S&P 500 than SPYI.

The reason TSPY has been able to accomplish this feat is because 0DTE options generally give the option sellers more opportunity to make profitable trades without sacrificing upside. Part of this is due to the fact that TSPY closes its trades by the end of the day and has overnight exposure to its underlying holdings. Studies have shown that overnight returns do often surpass intraday returns, which is why 0DTE funds like TSPY have actually been able to keep up with the underlying index in total returns.

Of course, this can go the other way as well. Just as how overnight returns can suddenly spike up, they can also fall significantly, and that is why 0DTE funds have essentially offered negligible downside protection. You can really see this when you compare TSPY with SPY and SPYI during April 2025. TSPY's options always expired by the end of the day, while SPYI's monthly options were still intact, and the value on the options can still help offset any losses in the underlying.

To visualize 0DTE's weaker downside protection and increased upside capture and see how the two funds may compare in different markets, we can take a look at both funds' trades. I already mentioned that SPYI has one call option with a strike at 6985 and another at 7055, both expiring February 20, 2026. For TSPY, I have collected its trade data for the week of January 5th, 2026.

Additionally, I have also provided open and close prices of the S&P 500 for the same time period below alongside total returns for TSPY, SPYI, and SPY:

The story that these tables and charts tell us is that on January 5th, TSPY made a profitable trade. Their option position expired worthless by the end of the day, and TSPY captured the full overnight growth in the SPX going to January 6th, plus the premiums. This is why TSPY actually outperformed SPY on January 6th. On the 6th, however, the SPX experienced significant growth that led to TSPY having to cash settle its position and take a loss. The money lost on this trade would show up the next day, where TSPY ultimately returned lower than SPY as the SPX also closed lower. Fortunately for TSPY, the next few days would be better, as the fund's trades were all profitable and gave the fund an opportunity to experience all the gains in the underlying. SPYI, on the other hand, sold monthly options. Being short these options makes them a liability on SPYI's balance sheets. As the underlying SPX moves closer toward or further away from SPYI's option strike prices, the liability of these options may increase or decrease, respectively, and it is reflected in the relatively more muted total returns.

While neither TSPY nor SPYI will have the exact same option trades every day or month, they will (outside extenuating circumstances) trade options daily and monthly. Closing option positions daily will mean that TSPY experiences essentially "naked" exposure to the underlying S&P 500. As you can see above, if the S&P 500 rises too suddenly during the day, it may force TSPY to close out its position and cap its upside. During the night, however, TSPY will experience the full changes from the previous close to the new day's open. SPYI, on the other hand, will experience more muted returns both up and down, as the value of its options always responds to price movements and affects SPYI's performance.

With this understanding, you can have a solid image of how TSPY's and SPYI's performances will compare against each other and the S&P 500 in various market circumstances, even without knowing the actual trades. In downward trending markets, TSPY will generally experience the full downside minus any premiums earned, while SPYI's options position can help offset unrealized losses. In upward-trending markets, both funds are capped by their options' strikes, but TSPY will be better off if the overnight returns are positive, as they experience full underlying exposure.

Why You Might Prefer TSPY

For most income investors, it is arguable that this extra volatility in TSPY compared to SPYI is well worth it. First, consider that both TSPY and SPYI are funds that will sell covered calls on a regular basis. Selling covered calls regularly is a strategy that will cap your upside in your underlying assets, and this upside cap has been regarded as the greatest risk in investing in these covered call ETFs. Take XYLD, a fund that sells monthly SPX covered calls with strike prices at-the-money ("ATM"), for example. XYLD is a fund that performed admirably in 2022 with total returns that outpaced and was less volatile than the S&P 500. In the bull markets of 2024 and 2025, however, the S&P 500 ultimately ended up doubling XYLD's returns as the fund capped its upside. At the same time, you should also note XYLD's NAV erosion, where its price returns have remained at nearly negative 20% since 2022.

No investor wants to see their investment on a constant decline, so the trend had shifted toward how a covered call fund might be able to capture more upside while still maintaining high yields. And so far, TSPY has demonstrated the most capability in achieving this with one of the highest total return levels relative to the S&P 500 and a yield averaging 13-14% since its inception.

As mentioned in my previous article on the NEOS S&P 500 Hedged Equity Income ETF (SPYH), income investors must also make a serious consideration of how important downside protection really is. Generally, the cases where you actually need real downside protection in your capital are very specific, where numbers are known. For example, you might have a loan backed by your assets, and your portfolio is only allowed to drop 10%. Otherwise, it becomes mostly just a peace of mind thing.

When it comes to comparing SPYI and TSPY, you'll find that SPYI's lower volatility does not ultimately change things that much at the end of the day. Since August 15, 2024, TSPY's rolling 1-month beta peaked at 1.2, while SPYI's was around 0.9, with the S&P 500 as the underlying asset. Both funds hold the S&P 500's constituents as their underlying holdings and will experience largely the same drops in value.

TSPY's drop may be slightly higher, while SPYI's may be slightly less. To put things in perspective, a 30% drop in SPY may result in a 36% drop in TSPY versus a 27% drop in SPYI. This lower volatility, however, does not necessarily justify taking lower upsides and dividend payouts. Most income investors are better off using TSPY's higher distributions to reinvest during downturns and grow their income stream faster, rather than relying on SPYI's extra downside protection.

And the discussion of volatility and TSPY's upside capture should be put in the broader market context. In 2026, pressing issues on hand already include Trump's strike on Venezuela, U.S.-Iran geopolitical tensions, and U.S. isolationist policies that have withdrawn the U.S. from many international organizations. Internally, judicial probes into Fed Chair Jerome Powell and a slowdown in earnings by AI-related companies point to 2026 being a highly volatile year. Major geopolitical events like Trump's initial tariff announcement in April 2025 may shock global markets, but they may just as equally prop up the market. Both the State Street Energy Select Sector SPDR ETF (XLE) and State Street Industrial Select Sector SPDR ETF (XLI) have already returned 3.65% and 5.19% year-to-date, following the Venezuela strike, as U.S. control over a country with one of the largest oil reserves and potential war could lead to increased economic potential for many companies within these sectors.

What this means for TSPY and SPYI is that during these times of increased volatility, when the market experiences both sudden rises and drops, SPYI will generally offer more muted returns, while TSPY will be able to capture more of the rebound that follows any market correction. Over the long term, TSPY should generally come out ahead so long as the U.S. economy and stock market do well.

Conclusion

All of this does not necessarily mean that I would sell SPYI and replace it with TSPY. The capital gains tax incursion does not make any sense, and there's always value in diversifying among fund providers. This is especially the case since SPYI has continued to be one of the best-performing S&P 500 covered call ETFs. That said, TSPY has demonstrated its worth as well, offering higher payouts and upside capture than SPYI at the cost of less downside protection. For most income investors, however, the higher upside is often worth the weaker downside protection. Neither fund is suitable as actual hedges in the first place, so the investor mentality is to understand that drops matching the S&P 500 will happen and should only serve as opportunities to reinvest at lower prices. I maintain a Strong Buy rating on TSPY and a Buy rating on SPYI.
